description: Default repositories and items seeded by the admin Knowledge Base 'Setup Defaults' button. Each collection maps 1:1 to an appointment service type. Repositories contain links to real public healthcare resources, sample announcements, and clinical notes.

repositories.name: Hygiene & Infection Control
repositories.description: Hand hygiene, PPE, infection prevention protocols for personal care aides.

items.title: CDC Hand Hygiene in Healthcare Settings
WHO Guidelines on Hand Hygiene in Health Care
Glove selection guide
items.content_text: Use nitrile gloves (not latex) for all personal care tasks. Check member file for latex allergy flag before each visit. Dispose after single use — never wash and reuse gloves.

items.title: Updated PPE policy effective May 2026
items.content_text: Effective May 1, 2026: all personal care aides must wear eye protection (face shield or goggles) during any task involving splash risk. Supplied in the field kit — request replacements via the mobile app.

repositories.name: Member Communication
repositories.description: Tips for building rapport, handling difficult conversations, cultural sensitivity.

items.title: Arrival routine — 5 steps
items.content_text: 1. Knock and announce yourself by name. 2. Show ID badge. 3. Confirm the member's identity (name + DOB). 4. Ask how they're feeling today. 5. Review today's care plan together before starting.

items.title: Handling refusal of care
items.content_text: If a member refuses a task: (1) acknowledge their autonomy, (2) ask if they'd like to try later, (3) document the refusal in visit notes, (4) do NOT force any activity. Escalate to care coordinator if refusal is repeated across visits.

repositories.name: Medication Reminders
repositories.description: Scope of practice for medication reminders (varies by state).

items.title: What aides CAN and CANNOT do
items.content_text: Personal care aides may REMIND members to take medications but may NOT administer, measure, or dispense. If a member asks you to open a pill bottle, hand them the bottle — do not place pills in their hand. Document all reminders in visit notes.

items.title: California AB-1234 — expanded aide scope
items.content_text: Effective Jan 2026, California home health aides may assist with pre-measured, pre-packaged oral medications under a registered nurse's delegation. Training required — see your coordinator.

repositories.name: Fall Prevention
repositories.description: Fall risk assessment, home safety checklists, post-fall protocols.

items.title: Home safety walkthrough checklist
items.content_text: On first visit: check for loose rugs, poor lighting, cluttered walkways, grab bars in bathroom, non-slip mats in shower/tub. Document findings in visit notes — care coordinator will arrange fixes if needed.

items.title: Post-fall protocol
items.content_text: 1. Do NOT move the member if injury is suspected. 2. Call 911 if unconscious, bleeding heavily, or unable to get up. 3. If member can stand safely, assist them to a chair. 4. Document the fall: time, location, activity, witnesses. 5. Call care coordinator immediately.

repositories.name: Q2 2026 Training Updates
repositories.description: Quarterly training materials and continuing education requirements.

items.title: Mandatory 4-hour refresher — due by June 30
items.content_text: All personal care aides must complete the Q2 refresher training by June 30, 2026. Topics: updated infection control, dementia care communication, fall prevention review. Schedule via the mobile app → Training tab.

items.title: Alzheimer's Association — Dementia Care Practice Recommendations
PHI National — Home Care Aide Training Resources

repositories.name: Wound Care Protocols
repositories.description: Wound assessment, dressing changes, documentation standards.

items.title: Wound documentation standard
items.content_text: Document: location (anatomical landmark), size (L×W×D in cm), stage (I-IV or unstageable), wound bed (granulation %, slough %, necrotic %), drainage (type, amount, odor), periwound skin condition. Use the PUSH tool score.

items.title: Dressing change frequency
items.content_text: Clean wounds: change every 48-72h or when soiled. Infected wounds: per physician order (typically daily). Always document the old dressing appearance before removing.

repositories.name: Medication Administration
repositories.description: The 6 Rights, high-alert medications, insulin protocols.

items.title: The 6 Rights of Medication Administration
items.content_text: 1. Right patient. 2. Right medication. 3. Right dose. 4. Right route. 5. Right time. 6. Right documentation. Verify all six BEFORE administering. If any doubt, call the prescribing physician.

items.title: ISMP High-Alert Medications in Community/Ambulatory Settings
Insulin pen safety reminder
items.content_text: NEVER share insulin pens between patients — even with a new needle. Each member must have their own pen. Report any suspected sharing to the care coordinator immediately.

repositories.name: Vital Signs & Assessment
repositories.description: Blood pressure, pulse, respiration, temperature — normal ranges and escalation.

items.title: Adult vital sign normal ranges
items.content_text: BP: 90/60 to 120/80 mmHg. Pulse: 60-100 bpm. Respirations: 12-20/min. Temp: 97.8-99.1°F (36.5-37.3°C). O2 sat: ≥95%. Document and escalate any value outside these ranges.

items.title: When to call the physician
items.content_text: Call immediately if: BP >180/120 or <80/50, pulse >120 or <50, temp >101.5°F, O2 sat <90%, acute confusion or consciousness change, new-onset chest pain or difficulty breathing.

repositories.name: IV Therapy & Central Lines
repositories.description: PICC line care, IV medication infusion, complications.

items.title: INS Infusion Therapy Standards of Practice
PICC dressing change protocol
items.content_text: Change PICC dressing every 7 days (transparent) or every 48h (gauze). Use chlorhexidine antiseptic. Document: insertion site appearance, catheter length external, dressing condition, patient tolerance.

items.title: Signs of line infection
items.content_text: Redness/warmth/swelling at insertion site, purulent drainage, fever >100.4°F, chills. If suspected: DO NOT remove the line — notify physician immediately for blood cultures and treatment.

repositories.name: Telehealth Integration
repositories.description: Conducting virtual assessments, technology requirements, documentation.

items.title: Pre-visit technology checklist
items.content_text: Before a telehealth visit: confirm internet connectivity, test video/audio, ensure member has a charged device, position camera for adequate lighting and visibility. Have emergency contact info ready in case of disconnection.

repositories.name: Exercise Programs
repositories.description: Standardized home exercise programs by condition.

items.title: Hip replacement — weeks 1-6 HEP
items.content_text: Ankle pumps (20 reps, 3x/day), quad sets (10 reps, 3x/day), heel slides (10 reps, 2x/day), standing hip abduction (10 reps when weight-bearing allowed). NO hip flexion >90°, NO crossing legs, NO internal rotation.

items.title: Balance progression sequence
items.content_text: Level 1: tandem stance (30s). Level 2: single-leg stance (15s). Level 3: tandem walk (10 steps). Level 4: single-leg stance on foam. Progress only when member can complete current level without loss of balance.

repositories.name: Pain Assessment
repositories.description: Pain scales, documentation requirements, escalation criteria.

items.title: Pain scale selection guide
items.content_text: Verbal patients: NRS (0-10 numeric rating scale). Non-verbal/cognitive impairment: FLACC (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability) or Wong-Baker FACES. Document scale used and score at each visit — trend over time.

items.title: NIH Pain Consortium — Resources for Clinicians

repositories.name: Assistive Device Training
repositories.description: Walker, cane, wheelchair — fitting, training, safety.

items.title: Walker fitting — the elbow test
items.content_text: Member stands inside the walker, arms at sides. Top of walker should align with the wrist crease. When gripping the handles, elbows should flex 15-20°. If too high: member leans forward (fall risk). If too low: shoulders elevate (fatigue/pain).

items.title: Stair navigation with cane
items.content_text: Going UP: good leg first, then cane + affected leg. Going DOWN: cane + affected leg first, then good leg. Mnemonic: 'Up with the good, down with the bad.' Always use the handrail when available.

repositories.name: ADL Assistance
repositories.description: Bathing, dressing, grooming, toileting — safe techniques.

items.title: Safe bathing sequence
items.content_text: 1. Check water temp (100-105°F). 2. Gather supplies before starting. 3. Wash face first (no soap), then arms, trunk, legs, perineal area last. 4. Use non-slip mat in tub/shower. 5. Never leave member unattended in water.

items.title: CMS Home Health Aide Competency Requirements
EVV compliance reminder — Q2 2026
items.content_text: All home health aide visits must have EVV (Electronic Visit Verification) check-in and check-out recorded via the mobile app. Missing EVV = denied claim. If the app fails, call the office within 1 hour to log a manual entry.

repositories.name: Nutrition & Meal Prep
repositories.description: Dietary restrictions, meal planning, food safety for home-bound members.

items.title: Common dietary restrictions quick reference
items.content_text: Diabetic: limit sugars, count carbs (45-60g/meal). Renal: limit sodium (<2g/day), potassium, phosphorus. Cardiac: low sodium (<1.5g/day), low saturated fat. Dysphagia: texture-modified (pureed, minced, soft) — check speech therapy orders.

items.title: Food safety — the 4 rules
items.content_text: 1. CLEAN: wash hands + surfaces often. 2. SEPARATE: don't cross-contaminate. 3. COOK: to safe internal temps. 4. CHILL: refrigerate within 2 hours. For home-bound members: always check expiration dates in the fridge/pantry.

repositories.name: Documentation & Reporting
repositories.description: Visit notes, incident reporting, mandatory reporter obligations.

items.title: Visit note essentials
items.content_text: Every visit note must include: arrival/departure time (EVV), tasks performed, member's condition/mood, any concerns or changes, vital signs if ordered, member's response to care. Use objective language — document what you SEE, not what you THINK.

items.title: Mandatory reporter obligations
items.content_text: Home health aides are MANDATORY REPORTERS of suspected abuse, neglect, or exploitation. Signs: unexplained injuries, fear of caregiver, poor hygiene, missing medications/funds. Report immediately to your supervisor AND your state's Adult Protective Services hotline. You are legally protected from retaliation.

repositories.name: Home Modification Assessments
repositories.description: Environmental assessment, adaptive equipment recommendations.

items.title: Kitchen safety assessment checklist
items.content_text: Check: stove knob guards, reachable storage (no overhead stretching), non-slip mats, adequate lighting, accessible fire extinguisher, easy-grip utensils available, microwave at counter height.

repositories.name: Cognitive Rehabilitation
repositories.description: Memory aids, executive function strategies, dementia care techniques.

items.title: Memory aid strategies
items.content_text: External aids: calendars, pill organizers, labeled drawers, written daily schedules, phone alarms. Internal aids: chunking (group related items), association (link new info to familiar), repetition (practice recall 3x). Start with external aids; add internal as cognition allows.

items.title: Alzheimer's Association — Activities for Dementia Care

repositories.name: Dysphagia Management
repositories.description: Swallowing assessments, diet texture levels, aspiration precautions.

items.title: IDDSI Framework — International Dysphagia Diet Standardisation
IDDSI levels quick reference
items.content_text: Level 0: Thin liquids. Level 1: Slightly thick. Level 2: Mildly thick. Level 3: Liquidised/moderately thick. Level 4: Pureed/extremely thick. Level 5: Minced & moist. Level 6: Soft & bite-sized. Level 7: Regular/easy to chew. Always match the member's prescribed level.

items.title: Aspiration precautions
items.content_text: Sit upright at 90° during meals and for 30 min after. Small bites, slow pace. No straws unless ordered. Alternate solids and liquids. Monitor for coughing, wet/gurgly voice, throat clearing during meals — signs of aspiration risk.

repositories.name: Aphasia Communication
repositories.description: Communication strategies for members with aphasia after stroke.

items.title: Talking to a member with aphasia
items.content_text: 1. Speak slowly and use short sentences. 2. Give extra time for responses (count to 10 in your head). 3. Use yes/no questions when possible. 4. Offer visual choices (point to pictures). 5. Don't finish their sentences. 6. Reduce background noise. 7. Write key words if speech is difficult.

repositories.name: Caregiver Transition Protocols
repositories.description: Handoff procedures, medication reconciliation, emergency contacts.

items.title: Caregiver handoff checklist
items.content_text: At start of respite shift: 1. Review care plan with primary caregiver. 2. Confirm medication schedule and last doses given. 3. Note dietary restrictions and meal times. 4. Get emergency contact numbers. 5. Tour the home (exits, bathroom, bedroom). 6. Ask about member's current mood and any recent changes.

items.title: End-of-shift documentation
items.content_text: Before primary caregiver returns: document all activities, meals, medications reminded/administered, any incidents, member's mood throughout. Verbal handoff to caregiver: highlight anything unusual.

repositories.name: Activity Planning
repositories.description: Engaging activities for members during respite visits.

items.title: Low-energy activity ideas
items.content_text: Reading aloud, puzzles, card games, music listening (ask about preferences), looking through photo albums, gentle chair exercises, gardening (container plants on the porch), watching a favorite show together.

items.title: Activities for members with dementia
items.content_text: Sorting tasks (buttons, coins, cards by color), folding towels, wiping tables, watering plants, singing familiar songs, looking at family photos. Match complexity to current cognitive level. Success > challenge. If frustrated, redirect.
